I also loved your array.slice animation. Very simple. Very expressive. And I learned something (the inserting part)!

text: I really like the "ghastly code" comment. One question, maybe I missed it in a previous video but what do you mean by a "whole new stack" for each function call? Is it really a whole new stack under the hood or just a logical stack for visualization?

text:
<@UCGAK10LS> (et al) — As far as current rules and guidelines go, this sort of discussion is fine for <#C01932BJGE8|present-company> as long as everyone keeps their cool. I find it interesting, but I'm opting to not participate because my political views are incendiary (or at least I like to think they are, haha). As a non-American, I'm with you that it would be nice to see more discussion of non-American politics, especially as it pertains to FoC (<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Project_Cybersyn|Project Cybersyn> comes to mind). But I view that as an "in addition to", not an "instead of" this sort of discussion.

It's good that you voiced your concern about the discussion. I'll take all the feedback I can get — just because I feel "fine" with it doesn't mean it's good for the health of the community. If you'd like to talk about it more, send me a DM or feel free to spin up a new discussion in <#CEXED56UR|administrivia>.

text:
> there are a lot of people in the “don’t know smalltalk but interested in GT” club in this very slack. maybe we should form a study group?
Count me in! I guess it is the good timing for me to discover both GT and Pharo/SmallTalk. When I came in this Community I have heard of GT, found it very interesting, but (temporarily) skipped it because of missing time. Now I have more time for stuff like that!

text: It does use a whole new stack. Though the distinction with "just visualization" is kinda moot when there are no side effects. Evaluation is a tiny part of visualization here, and evaluation often happens over and over again for a single rendering of the screen. Either way, there's no way for a function to access the (caller's) stack below the args it declares.

text: I was able to get back to my uCISC processor work this week. I went back to the drawing board a bit and reworked my instruction encoding a bit, making more room for immediate values. Also, new JVM based vm for uCISC with tests written in uCode. I realize micro-code overloads the term a bit, but I didn't realize that until after I made the video. The name is a WIP :joy: <https://youtu.be/YEcWild_3vA>

attachments.title: Two Minute Week #3

text:
We will organize a set of live session in the GT Discord community. The next one is an introductory session and will be held on Tuesday, October 13, 5PM CET: <https://discord.gg/FTJr9gP>

You are all welcome to join.

Of course, if you’d want to have a dedicated channel here, we’d be happy to offer guidance here as well.

text: I do object a bit here <@UC2A2ARPT>. I do understand that the matter has some relations with present ongoing election and political scene in America but I also firmly believe that it represents a side of technology that we should all be very cautious about. This is a powerhouse of bad things and Future of Coding does not only mean Technological or Design advances in computer science but it also means ethically better computing. Don't you agree? I am a non-american and I posted this here for that reason. Not to discuss or ignite a discussion about American politics rather to discuss what FoC can do to prevent such kind of sources.

text:
<@UPVBV34EL>

Let's split this into a few pieces.

• The American vs non stuff is mostly in reference to Kartik's first post and Nick's post, not the rest of the thread.
• The question of relevance to FoC is mostly a matter of framing. We could have an FoC-oriented discussion on the subject (or, really, any subject). But you began this thread with "Not directly FoC but I guess highly important", which isn't much of a prompt for any particular kind of discussion. If you'd like to point the conversation in a new direction, by all means go for it. Tell us a little more of what you think about the ideas in article.

text: I agree on that <@UC2A2ARPT> that my original post wordings were poorly chosen. And I probably should have written something about how I feel this is a growing problem for the time to come. I grew up in an Analog First world in India in 1990's and the more the world is becoming digital first the more strange problem are arising which we are very poorly equipped to solve at the moment. I sometimes feel that often we do not understand the true implications of a particular innovation in its full breadth and depth.

text:
love the visualization! checking my understanding about intended semantics: if i defined a function as `x y myFunc: x y + 4 x *;` would it pull two values off the stack, with the x getting the top value and y getting the one below it?

also (tricky question): any thoughts on recursion?

text:
I haven't really used multiple args yet, so not sure if I've done it right. But my thought is that arg order should match calls. The function definition `x y myFunc` should map to a call like `3 4 myFunc` with `x` getting `3` and `y` getting `4`.

Perhaps I'm missing something, but I don't think there's anything tricky about recursion! A recursive call is just a function call like any other, and the visualization metaphor here should uncoil it just like any other call. I don't have conditionals or quotes yet, so all recursion at the moment would be infinite. So it's not very useful at the moment.

text: One concern I have with this whole approach is something I've chatted with <@UC2A2ARPT> about before and something visible in the screenshot above: lots of name-punning. Different parts of a single (expanded) line may have very different behavior attached to the same name (here `x`).
